item.title: Relaxing or imposing constraints? Exploring the associations between digitalization and space-time flexibilities in Qinghe, Beijing
item.description:
Authors: Jingying Liao, Chunjiang Li, Yanwei Chai

Source: Journal of Transport Geography

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: While information and communications technology (ICT)-based multitasking has become a routine feature of daily activity organization, its implications for space-time constraints remain insufficiently understood. This study develops ICT-based multitasking by distinguishing foreground and background ICT use and examines how different forms of ICT use are associated with perceived temporal and spatial flexibility at the activity and daily levels. The analysis draws on a 2-day combined daily activity diary and internet activity diary survey conducted in 2021 in Qinghe, Beijing. In particular, records from both diaries are integrated to distinguish background and foreground ICT use. The associations of foreground and background ICT use with space-time flexibility are estimated by multilevel ordered logistic regression at the activity level and ordinary least squares regression with standard...

item.title: Optimization of graphene nanoplatelet incorporation in cementitious composites using Taguchi and response surface method
item.description:
Authors: Maria Liovas, Reagan Farough, Adeyemi Adesina

Source: Next Nanotechnology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) have shown potential to improve the performance of cementitious composites, but their effectiveness strongly depends on dosage and dispersion quality. This study investigates the combined influence of GNP dosage and ultrasonication time on the mechanical, durability, and microstructural properties of cementitious composites. A Taguchi L9 orthogonal array was used to design mixtures containing 0.01%, 0.05%, and 0.10% GNP by mass of cement, dispersed by ultrasonication for 5, 10, and 20 min. A control mixture without GNPs was also prepared. The mixtures were evaluated using 7- and 28-day compressive strength, water absorption, permeable voids, scanning electron microscopy, signal-to-noise ratio analysis, analysis of variance, and response surface modelling. The results showed that the mixture containing 0.01% GNP and 20 min ultrasonication achieved the...

item.title: Impact of Gaussian feathering on diagnostic metrics in tile-based micro-CT sinogram infilling
item.description:
Authors: Falk L. Wiegmann, Nancy Ford

Source: Scientific Reports

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Abstract Tile-based sinogram infilling methods for micro-CT require merging overlapping output tiles to reconstruct full images. Gaussian feathering, the standard blending approach, produces visually seamless results but its effect on diagnostic image quality metrics has not been characterized. This study quantifies how Gaussian feathering affects noise power spectrum (NPS), modulation transfer function (MTF), noise equivalent quanta (NEQ), structural similarity index (SSIM), and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) compared to nearest priority blending, also known as Voronoi partition blending. We mathematically derived the variance reduction mechanism in Gaussian blending and experimentally compared both methods using sinograms infilled by DeepFill v2 as a representative generative inpainting model, applied to a micro-CT quality assurance phantom reconstructed from 50% undersampled data....

item.title: Detectable Human Influence on Daytime-Nighttime Compound Heat Waves over China
item.description:
Authors: Wenxin Xie, Botao Zhou, Qiaohong Sun, Yan Li, Xuebin Zhang

Source: Advances in Atmospheric Sciences

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Abstract Daytime-nighttime compound heat waves (CHWs) pose severe threats to human society. In the context of climate warming, China has experienced significant increases and intensification of CHWs since the 1960s. This article performs a detection and attribution analysis on observed changes in CHWs over China and within its four subregions: northwestern China, northeastern China, the Yangtze River valley, and southern China, based on Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) simulations. Notably, an updated optimal fingerprinting approach with estimating equations is utilized, which is improved by more efficient point estimation and more plausible uncertainty evaluation. In general, the findings highlight human influence as the primary contributor to aggravated CHWs in China. The anthropogenic (ANT) signal can be detected and separated from natural-only (NAT) forcing in...

item.title: Transgender and gender diverse persons’ sexually transmitted infection testing: a mixed methods study to understand primary care providers’ training gaps and desires to promote sexual health equity
item.description:
Authors: Ashley Lacombe‐Duncan, Seema Mehta, Shanna K. Kattari, Rory P. O’Brien, Hadas Kluger, Julie Blaszczak

Source: BMC Health Services Research

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Transgender (trans) and gender diverse (TGD) people experience sexual health inequities, including high rates of HIV and other sexually transmitted infections (STIs), driven by anti-trans stigma. Yet, TGD people also face barriers to STI testing related to anti-trans stigma, including a lack of access to knowledgeable and affirming primary care providers (PCPs), including physicians, nurse practitioners, and physician assistants. We sought to understand the current practices, comfort, and knowledge gaps of PCPs in performing gender-affirming STI testing, particularly non-HIV STI testing, among TGD persons, in order to inform future educational interventions. We utilized a convergent parallel mixed-methods study design drawing on cross-sectional quantitative surveys ( n = 50) and semi-structured qualitative interviews ( n = 8) with PCPs (2024–2025). The survey assessed sociodemographic...

item.title: The embryo game uncovers hidden cell behaviours
item.description:
Authors: Maria Abou Chakra, Joshua Hislop, Ipek Egilmez, Rafael Alkalai, Omar Bashth, Kieran Maheden, Akash Gundagathi, Mo R. Ebrahimkhani et al.

Source: Nature Communications

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: While cells are driven to preserve their own survival, they must cooperate to ensure embryo viability. To explore how cellular behaviours shape human development, we intersect evolutionary game theory and in vitro embryo modelling. We focus on cell competition, a conserved cell-cell killing thought to enable embryonic error correction, though few studies directly link it to embryo success. Our collective risk game shows that killing is costly for a cell, but beneficial when the embryo exceeds its target size and the probability of survival is low. Analogously, successful heX-embryoids form within the target size range of the native human embryonic disc and inhibiting cell death produces larger, defective embryoids. Killing and proliferating are responsive cellular behaviours that maintain optimal embryoid size, consistent with their emergence as conditional strategies in our game....

item.title: Kelponomics: The state of the North American kelp economy
item.description:
Authors: Sarah B Gutzmann, Joshua Russell, Samantha Martinez de Arredondo Raya, Andrés M. Cisneros-Montemayor

Source: Journal of Applied Phycology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, canadian

Abstract: The North American kelp sector is growing in Canada, the USA, and Mexico. In part, this expansion is due to the enthusiasm around the sector’s promise for environmental, social, economic, and climate benefits. However, these Blue Economy goals may not be inherently realized by Indigenous Nations and coastal communities where kelp industries operate. To investigate the status of the kelp-economy in North America, we compiled a database of 300 kelp-related organizations from across North America and gave each a score from 1–4 for community participation as a proxy for community-derived benefit. We then looked at broader political, economic, social, technological, legal, and environmental (PESTLE) factors at the state/province level to evaluate which enabled community participation. Finally, we used a cluster analysis to group states/provinces into broader regions based on their PESTLE...

item.title: Clinical Correlates of Resting-State Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging in Military Personnel with Adulthood—Onset War—Related Post—Traumatic Stress Disorder
item.description:
Authors: Olivier Roy, Jean Levasseur‐Moreau, Emmanuelle Renauld, Marine Mondino, Luc J. Hébert, Jean-Martin Beaulieu, Mathieu Bilodeau, Shirley Fecteau

Source: Brain Connectivity

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: BACKGROUND: Investigation of the neural substrates of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in military personnel using whole-brain approaches remains scarce, hindering the development of circuit-based neuromodulatory interventions. OBJECTIVES: This study aimed to identify potential associations between clinical symptoms and whole-brain resting-state functional connectivity with magnetic resonance imaging in military personnel with adulthood-onset war-related PTSD. METHODS: Thirty-seven soldiers from the Canadian Armed Forces with moderate to severe treatment-resistant PTSD participated in this study. We assessed PTSD, anxiety and depressive symptoms, quality of life, and time since trauma. We characterized the whole-brain functional connectome using independent component analysis and regions of interest (ROI)-to-ROI connectivity, as well as its topology using graph theory. RESULTS: <...

item.title: Midwifery-led care models across health systems: a scoping review protocol of organisational arrangements and core components
item.description:
Authors: Manuela Lopes, R Moreira, Florence D’haenens, Anna Martín-Arribas, Cátia Santos, Conceição Moreira Freitas, Francisco J. Soriano-Vidal, Ines Rothmann et al.

Source: Open Research Europe

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: inuit

Abstract: <ns3:p>Objective To map and characterize organisational models of midwifery-led care across healthcare systems, identifying their organisational arrangements and core and adaptable components to inform cross-country comparison and contextual adaptation. Introduction Midwifery-led care models are increasingly recognized as effective approaches to improving maternal and newborn outcomes and women’s experiences of care while promoting continuity of care, person-centred maternity services, and sustainable health systems. However, substantial heterogeneity exists in how these models are organised, implemented, and integrated across different health systems. A systematic mapping of organisational arrangements and their contextual conditions is needed to support evidence-informed adaptation and implementation across diverse settings. Inclusion criteria This review will consider sources...

item.title: Feedback focus: An exploration of documented feedback conversations with episodic versus continuous supervisors
item.description:
Authors: Ann Lee, Kelsey Compagna, Pascal W. M. Van Gerven, Sylvia Heeneman, Shelley Ross

Source: Medical Teacher

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: Purpose Feedback conversations are essential to learning, and the relationship between supervisor and learner plays a role in the effectiveness of feedback shared. Documentation of these conversations in learner portfolios is intended to support reflection, learning, and growth. However, there is limited understanding of what gets documented after feedback conversations. Guided by Hattie and Timperley’s model for feedback, we examined documented feedback conversations with episodic and continuous supervisors to better understand the information contained in learner portfolios.Methods The authors examined archived de-identified FieldNotes (FNs; workplace-based assessment forms) completed for residents located at two teaching sites in a Canadian university-based family medicine residency program. The content of written feedback on each FN was coded for feedback focus (Task, Process,...

item.title: Deep Learning Sewer Defects Detection: Comparison of Multilabel Utility-Specific Classification ModelsBased on Convolutional Neural Networks
item.description:
Authors: Vannary Seng, Barbara J. Lence, Sudhir Kshirsagar, Deven M. Mistry, Pavan Saranguhewa

Source: Journal of Infrastructure Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: Closed-circuit television (CCTV) is widely employed for identifying sewer defects. This comparative study develops utility-specific multilabel classification models for three communities in Western Canada (Utilities A-C), based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), for identifying multiple sewer defects in CCTV video images. The models are trained, validated, and tested with frames extracted from CCTV videos. They are tailored for the different characteristics of the individual utilities and explored to evaluate the benefit of transfer learning. For each utility, we examine three existing CNN backbone architectures, previously trained on the ImageNet data set: MobileNet V3 Large, ResNet50, and ResNet101; two model configurations: one-stage for which all defects and nondefects are classified in one model, and two-stage for which nondefects and defects are sorted first, and then all...

item.title: Workaround behaviors: nurses’ strategies for coping with high workload and burnout
item.description:
Authors: Sari Mansour, Marie-Ève Beauchamp Legault, Denis Chênevert

Source: Journal of Health Organization and Management

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, quebec

Abstract: Purpose Healthcare workers often develop particular work patterns that can improve their performance and patient safety. These working methods are known as “workarounds” and can be defined as alternative work procedures developed by employees to solve workflow obstacles or problems without necessarily analyzing the underlying causes. Studies on workarounds have mainly been concerned with documenting their existence, and few studies have looked at the potential antecedents of workarounds or their impacts. Building on previous research and the Conservation of Resources theory, this study aims to examine the effects of workload on workaround behaviors using the three components of burnout (emotional exhaustion, physical fatigue and cognitive exhaustion) as mediators. Design/methodology/approach Cross-sectional data from 562 nurses in Quebec (a province of Canada) were analyzed. Structural...

item.title: First report of tenacibaculosis in wild-caught Pacific salmon
item.description:
Authors: Emiliano Di Cicco, Sean C. Godwin, Kaitlyn R. Zinn, Stephen D. Johnston, Karia H. Kaukinen, Angela D. Schulze, Shaorong Li, Jonathan F. Archambault et al.

Source: Scientific Reports

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: Understanding disease dynamics in wild fish populations is essential for effective conservation and fisheries management, yet diagnosing marine diseases in wild organisms is challenging. Tenacibaculosis, a bacterial disease caused by Tenacibaculum spp., is emblematic of this challenge, having been widely reported in farmed Atlantic ( Salmo salar) and Chinook ( Oncorhynchus tshawytscha ) salmon, but never confirmed in any species of wild Pacific salmon ( Oncorhynchus spp.). Here, we present the first report of tenacibaculosis in wild-caught Pacific salmon. During holding experiments conducted with Chinook salmon captured in Barkley Sound, Canada in 2022, we observed external lesions in 16 of 99 fish and associated high mortality. Through molecular screening, analysis of genetic sequences, and microscopic localisation of the infectious agents present, we identified Tenacibaculum...

item.title: Short-term responses of breeding songbirds to a gradient of broadleaf removal in managed mixedwood forests in British Columbia, Canada
item.description:
Authors: Leonard Patterson, Juan Andrés Martínez‐Lanfranco, Ryan Jackalin, Erin Hall, Kari Stuart-Smith, Erin M. Bayne

Source: Forest Ecology and Management

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: canada, british columbia; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, british columbia

Abstract: Brushing is a silvicultural practice that reduces regenerating broadleaf vegetation in young conifer plantations. Despite its widespread use, the effect of brushing on breeding songbirds is poorly understood, especially across a gradient of broadleaf retention. We evaluated how upland forest-associated songbirds respond to four broadleaf retention treatments—control (no broadleaf removal), high retention, low retention, and full removal—using 6 randomized treatment blocks near Prince George, British Columbia, Canada. We collected automated acoustic point counts for one year pre-treatment (2023) and two years post-treatment (2024 and 2025) in stands ranging from 7–10 years post-planting. We used a Before-After Control-Impact design to assess change in community diversity (alpha, gamma, and beta diversity) and species-level relative abundance using two point count methodologies (truncated...

item.title: Mental Resilience and Community Connections: A Case Study of Ukrainian Scholars’ Adaptation Experiences in Canada
item.description:
Authors: Yulia Ivaniuk Squires, Sofiya Stavkova, Лілія Клос, Maureen Flaherty

Source: Mental Health Global Challenges Journal

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: canada; Canada-related term in abstract: manitoba; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: To read the full-text Open Access article and download the PDFs, please visit our Issue portal at Paradigm (Walter de Gruyter): https://reference-global.com/article/10.56508/mhgcj.v9i1.364 Introduction: War and natural disasters have forced many people to migrate, requiring considerable adaptation of the migrants and the communities which receive them. Purpose: This case study explores the contributors to mental resilience for Ukrainian scholars who fled Russia’s full-scale war against Ukraine taking up temporary appointments at the University of Manitoba through supports through CUAET and Scholar-at-Risk pathways. Methodology: Grounded in appreciative inquiry, the study uses mixed qualitative sources. The empirical base includes the co-authors’ reflexive accounts as displaced scholars, informal conversations with scholar-peers, local media interviews, public social media posts, and...

item.title: Active Learning In Systematic Literature Screening: Benchmarking Ai-Assisted Screening Against Human Consensus
item.description:
Authors: Mickael Ringeval, Guy Paré, Grégory Vial, Aude Motulsky

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Active Learning (AL) tools are increasingly used to assist the title and abstract screening phase of systematic literature reviews, with the promise of reducing workload while maintaining comprehensiveness. However, empirical evidence regarding their effectiveness relative to validated human screening decisions remains limited. This study conducts an exploratory benchmarking evaluation of AI-assisted screening by comparing two widely used AI-enabled tools, ASReview and Covidence, against a validated human consensus dataset derived from a published meta-synthesis. Performance was assessed using recall, screening duration, the number of full-text review candidates (FTRC), and workload reduction. Manual screening of 5,808 references required 1,018 minutes and resulted in 92 FTRC and a final sample of 28 studies. Using the same dataset, ASReview achieved full recall relative to the manual...

item.title: Synthetic Data Generation Using Llms For Hate Speech Detection In Political Posts
item.description:
Authors: Tintu Thankom Koshy, Omar Ahmed, noorian, zeinab, noorian, Amira Ghenai

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Hate speech detection in political discourse is hindered by the scarcity of domain-specific hate examples and severe class imbalance in election-related data. To address this challenge, we develop a topic-aware synthetic data generation pipeline that uses large language models to produce contextually grounded hate-speech samples aligned with discourse from the 2024 U.S. election. We manually annotate 6,499 tweets, apply BERTopic to identify thematic structure, and generate synthetic hate tweets conditioned on representative examples and topic-level cues. These synthetic samples are combined with the original dataset to fine-tune transformer-based classifiers. The augmented dataset yields significant improvements in hate-speech detection, with the best-performing model increasing its Hate-class F1 score from 0.67 to 0.88 after augmentation. These findings demonstrate that LLM-generated...

item.title: A Context-Aware Urban Heat Companion For Prosumer Participatory Sensing And Just-In-Time Adaptive Intervention
item.description:
Authors: Niklas Heyden, Florian Onur Kuhlmeier, Jeffrey Parsons, Alexander Maedche

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Extreme heat poses increasing risks to urban populations, yet current monitoring infrastructures fail to capture local micro-climate variations and the individual human context of heat exposure. This study applies a Design Science Research (DSR) approach to develop and evaluate the Heat Companion, a context-aware participatory sensing system that integrates wearable environmental sensors, a mobile application, and on-device adaptive feedback. Grounded in Prosumer Theory and the Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (JITAIs) framework, the system enables citizens to act simultaneously as data producers and beneficiaries of context-specific recommendations. A two-week field study demonstrated the artifact’s technical reliability, usability, and capacity to sustain engagement through adaptive reciprocal feedback. Our insights refine six design requirements for citizen-centric Green...

item.title: Before The Leap: Collective Sensemaking Of Artificial Intelligence In Digital Platform Ecosystems
item.description:
Authors: Sumin Song, Arman Sadreddin

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming digital commerce, yet entrepreneurs in this space typically encounter AI as platform-mediated discourse rather than as a working technology. This study examines how entrepreneurs make sense of AI within a digital platform ecosystem prior to adoption by analyzing 3,195 public messages across 385 discussion threads from Shopify, a leading e-commerce platform. Using a computationally assisted approach, we identify three stages of collective sensemaking: 1) exposure, 2) exploration, and 3) evaluation. Our analysis shows that entrepreneurs predominantly frame AI as augmentative, while automation-oriented interpretations are less common. These processes suggest that AI diffusion in platform ecosystems is shaped by interpretive work in which discursive cues, interface behaviors, and peer exchanges guide expectations before adoption occurs....

item.title: Anticipating Ethical Issues Of Recommender Systems: An Exploratory Study In The Insurance Domain
item.description:
Authors: Ines Aouadi, Sehl Mellouli, Thierry Badard

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This research in progress examines ethical issues in insurance recommender systems, where recommendations can influence financial protection. RSs are well established in e-commerce and entertainment, but insurance presents specific ethical issues that are less explored. We used a reflexive thematic analysis to highlight ethical issues before any large RS deployment in insurance companies. To this end, we conducted semi-structured interviews with insurance, legal, and ethical professionals to anticipate issues as perceived by these professionals. Early findings show four key patterns: regulatory gaps between current legal frameworks and RS capabilities, opacity in technical processes that obscures decision-making, the transfer of digital trust from consumer platforms to insurance decisions, and the conflict between client needs and organizational financial objectives. These results show...

item.title: To Tell Or Not To Tell? Managing Self-Disclosure Boundaries In Online Health Communities
item.description:
Authors: Assia Lasfer

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Online peer support communities help patients access social support and empowerment, yet these public spaces expose personal information and emotions, raising questions about how members protect their privacy and vulnerability. Drawing on communication privacy management theory, we develop an individual-level model of self-disclosure based on qualitative data from 11 interviews and public trace data from two Facebook support groups. We argue that individuals experience tension as they balance privacy and disclosure, resolving it through boundary management configurations of audience, information, and emotions, which is enacted through digital communication. Configuring boundary management is guided by rules individuals build while engaging in the community, which consist of an assessment of contextual appropriateness, risk–benefit trade-offs, and emotional need. Self-disclosure is also...

item.title: A Low-Threshold Digital Intervention To Enhance Menstrual Health Awareness And Literacy In The Workplace
item.description:
Authors: Davinny Sou, Vassilka Kavassakali, Saeed Amirabdolahian, Achim Reuther, Tobias Kowatsch, Marcia Nißen

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Menstrual health is essential to employee well-being and organizational performance, yet stigma and silence continue to restrict communication and support in workplaces. Despite clear impacts on productivity, absenteeism, and presenteeism, menstrual health remains largely absent from organizational well-being initiatives. Digital interventions offer a scalable, low-threshold opportunity to address these gaps; however, such interventions targeting menstrual health in workplace settings remain underexplored. This study examines whether a low-threshold digital intervention combining a reflective scenario with a menstrual health knowledge quiz, can (1) improve employees’ intentions to communicate openly about menstrual health and (2) reduce stigma while increasing actual communication behavior. We employ a single-arm pre-post study in a large German company, assessing changes in perceived...

item.title: Navigating Ai Choices: A Taxonomy Of Decision Factors On Ai Use Case Selection
item.description:
Authors: Kathrin Weber, Grégory Vial, Dennis M. Steininger

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is expected to be the primary driver of economic growth over the next decade across industries. However, many organizations face uncertainty in implementing AI, as selecting appropriate use cases among numerous opportunities poses unique challenges. Existing research focuses on AI’s technological foundations, while limited attention has been paid to the use case decision process followed by organizations. Since such decisions have a significant impact on the success of AI initiatives, our study develops a taxonomy for structuring important decision criteria based on a literature review, a case study, and expert interviews. Our taxonomy consists of three levels that focus on organizational, technical, and skills-based factors. With this taxonomy, our work aims to contribute to nascent knowledge on decision-making in the AI implementation literature. The next...

item.title: From Brown To Green: How Ai-Driven Transformation Enables Business Model Reconfiguration In The Twin Transition
item.description:
Authors: Elaine Mosconi, Marie‐Claude Boudreau

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: The twin transition links digital transformation and sustainability, yet the role of artificial intelligence (AI) in supporting sustainability-oriented business model reconfiguration remains underexplored in Information Systems research. This paper examines how an AI-enabled platform supports such reconfiguration through a qualitative case study of a Canadian engineering firm, EnginiCo. Drawing on interviews, observations, and documentary data, we show how the firm’s AI platform, OptiMesh, supported a shift from transactional consulting to recurring, outcome-oriented services centered on asset durability, lifecycle performance, and sustainability value. Our findings highlight four dimensions of EnginiCo’s AI-enabled digital transformation: strategic drivers and framing, AI-enabled business model reconfiguration, governance and capability building, and trade-offs and constraints. These...

item.title: The SIMPLE Archive
item.description:
Authors: William Cooper, Kelle L. Cruz, David R. Rodriguez, Jonathan Gagné

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: We present the SIMPLE Archive of low mass stars, brown dwarfs and directly-imaged exoplanets. The SIMPLE Archive currently contains the astrometry, photometry and spectra for over 3600 sources. The goal of SIMPLE is to develop a community-driven archive where many individuals can collaboratively contribute to the knowledge base of these objects using a GitHub, hand-curated workflow. SIMPLE can be searched via a website, https://simple-bd-archive.org/, or be downloaded and interacted with as a database. We welcome more contributors to join us as we build and populate this community tool. The SIMPLE archive is actively being devloped on GitHub at https://github.com/SIMPLE-AstroDB. Breaking news: We are structuring SIMPLE to be fully complementary and compatible with MocaDB, which is considerably larger and more automated than SIMPLE.

item.title: Human–Generative Ai Coordination And The Dynamics Of Artistic Style Evolution
item.description:
Authors: Sepide Sadeghi

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence is changing the way visual art and illustration are produced.Prior research has focused primarily on individual-level human-AI collaboration, offering limited insight into how collective stylistic patterns emerge when many creators interact through shared generative tools and visible outputs.To address this gap, we develop an agent-based model that captures how creators balance exploration and AI-assisted exploitation while responding to visible styles.Our preliminary results suggest that the impact of generative AI on stylistic diversity depends critically on the level of human exploration: AI-driven convergence emerges primarily when exploration is limited, whereas higher levels of exploration sustain diversity over time.By shifting attention from individual outcomes to ecosystem-level dynamics, this study provides new insight into how widespread...

item.title: Designing For The Co-Creation Triad: Design Principles For Agentic Ai In Configured Digital Services
item.description:
Authors: Shahper Richter, Marilyn Giroux, Tuure Tuunanen, Shohil Kishore

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

item.title: Pathways To Entrepreneurial Ecosystem Connectedness: A Configurational Approach
item.description:
Authors: Mohammad Qorbani Hesari, Arman Sadreddin

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Entrepreneurial ecosystems (EEs) are complex environments that drive entrepreneurship and regional development.In these ecosystems, digital technologies are defined as crucial components enabling coordination and connectivity within the ecosystem.Drawing on Complex Adaptive Systems (CAS) to explain this complexity, we focus on the connectedness among actors as a key goal of ecosystems and as essential for breaking down siloed activities.While prior studies offer rich insights from qualitative case studies, they often lack a comparative lens across diverse contexts.This study addresses that gap by applying Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) as a configurational approach to compare published EE case studies.In this study, we identify recurring patterns based on combinations of digital technology characteristics, involvement of actors, and ecosystem maturity.These patterns explain how...

item.title: Informal Communication And Collaboration: The Case Of Electronic Health Record Implementation
item.description:
Authors: Alexander Peever

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The implementation of electronic health records continues in healthcare settings, yet significant implementation failures persist.This prospective research draws insights from a recently troubled implementation, which identified communication failures, such as ignoring informal channels, as one of the factors leading to the project's failure.This qualitative study explores how informal communication within EHR implementation projects impacts collaboration and project success.The lens of transactive memory systems is used as an initial frame to guide data collection.It is proposed that ignoring or making informal channels inaccessible to core team members may deprive them of team-related knowledge, impair their ability to coordinate and collaborate, and jeopardize the project.The case study method is outlined.

item.title: A Roadmap For Governing The Mlops Chain
item.description:
Authors: Elisa Gagnon, Elaine Mosconi, Anita Neumannová

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Machine-learning operations (MLOps) encompass practices and tools that operationalize the development of machine-learning (ML) solutions throughout their lifecycle.As such, the governance of MLOps chains is inherently complex, requiring adequate operationalization across multiple teams and applications, which has yet to be reflected in extant scholarly research.Through this research, we aim to contribute to this discourse by identifying the governance mechanisms that sustain ML solutions in production over time, drawing on design science research within a large organization.

item.title: Unmasking Unsolicited Text Messages Through Linguistic Markers
item.description:
Authors: Stephen Jackson, Nadia Vanteeva

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Unsolicited communication (spam) presents a growing concern for individuals, organizations and societies.While many types of spam exist, for instance, email, social media, and voice, the focus of this study is on instant messaging spam delivered via short messaging service (SMS).Although much research has focused on spam detection over the last several decades, gaps remain in fully understanding the linguistic markers that separate spam from genuine communication (ham).Drawing on impression management theory and using 10,409 observations, four key linguistic cues (cognitive processes, negative tone, personal pronouns, and authenticity) that distinguish ham from spam communication are identified.More specifically, the lower the cognitive processes, authenticity, negative tone, and use of 1st singular and 1st plural pronouns, the more likely the text message is to be spam.The study...

item.title: Low-Code/No-Code Development Platforms In Entrepreneurial Ecosystems
item.description:
Authors: Arman Sadreddin, Alireza Khaleghi-Songhor

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Low-code/no-code development platforms represent an emerging technological trend in entrepreneurial ecosystems, offering the potential to democratize innovation.Despite their growing relevance, academic research on this phenomenon remains limited.In this study, we conducted a scoping review of the current literature on how actors in entrepreneurial ecosystems leverage these technologies.Only four studies met the inclusion criteria, highlighting the underexplored nature of this research area.The review synthesizes existing evidence.This work contributes to the digital entrepreneurship literature by providing an early systematic mapping of the area and laying the groundwork for further theoretical and empirical advancements on the role of digital technology in entrepreneurial ecosystems.

item.title: Anticipating The Invisible: Exploring How Organizations Operationalize Dark Web Intelligence For Physical Security
item.description:
Authors: Michelle Virgilio, Mahdi Mirhoseini, Arman Sadreddin

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study examines how organizations use dark web intelligence to support physical security decisionmaking. While prior research has focused mainly on technical approaches such as data collection and machine learning, less attention has been given to how organizations interpret and use this information in practice. Using an exploratory qualitative multiple case study approach based on interviews with security professionals, this study identifies key barriers, including unclear responsibilities, limited analytical capabilities and lack of structured processes. The findings explain how organizations translate digital threat signals into preventive actions and highlight the importance of coordination and decision-making practices in supporting physical security.

item.title: Theme Park Audiovisual Aesthetics in Cozy Games and Acoustic Territory-Making for Micro-Scaled Cozy Worlds
item.description:
Authors: Kate Galloway

Source: Journal of International Crisis and Risk Communication Research

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Cozy video games such as Animal Crossing: New Horizons (Nintendo 2020) and Stardew Valley (ConcernedApe 2016) draw extensively from theme park design principles to craft experiences grounded in comfort, predictability, and sensory pleasure. Like theme parks, these games rely on spatial legibility, choreographed movement, controlled temporality, and carefully curated aesthetics. Their audiovisual landscapes featuring soft color palettes, miniaturized architecture, forced perspective, and clearly defined pathways echo the designed micro-worlds of parks, while looping tasks and repeatable activities mirror ride cycles and daily routines that encourage rhythmic engagement. Although often described through tropes of cuteness, pastoralism, or nostalgia, cozy games also operate as immersive, emotionally legible environments structured through multimodal theming, especially sound. Theme parks...

item.title: The Human Oversight approaches at the forefront of responsible and trustworthy AI, from data-centric adaptive AI-Ops pipelines to Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
Authors: Marina Da Bormida in Cugurra, André Grilo, Carlos Agostinho, Sotirios Koussouris, André Tabone, Marta Csanalosi Artigas, Antonia Zumblick, Theodore Dalamagas et al.

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The paper focuses on human oversight mechanisms in Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems and their pipelines, with particular attention to Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) and Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) approaches. It explores their main advantages and challenges over fully autonomous systems, from a technical, legal and ethical perspective. The study discusses how the adoption of human judgment and feedback can address critical gaps in accuracy, accountability, fairness, transparency, and trust, especially when combined with explainable AI (XAI) techniques. However, such a human oversight paradigm can also raise issues, including scalability constraints, human error, cognitive load and fatigue, as well as biases in training data, privacy risks, “rubber stamping”, or even legal liability. These concepts, advantages and challenges are are first analysed through a literature review, and then further...

item.title: Hybrid Model Engineering: A Residual Learning Approach for Modular AI Pipelines
item.description:
Authors: Theodora Chrysoula, Viktor Daropoulos, André Grilo, Vasilis Gkolemis, Anargiros Tzerefos, Eleni Lavasa, Theodore Dalamagas, Stratos Keranidis et al.

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Hybrid models combine first-principles knowledge with machine learning to enhance predictive performance while preserving physical consistency and interpretability. Despite their advantages, such approaches are often developed in a problem specific manner and lack standardized workflows that support reuse and systematic experimentation. To address this challenge, this work proposes a modular, pipeline-based framework for hybrid model engineering within the Data Analytics and Visualization Environment (DAVE). The proposed Hybrid Model Engineering Engine (HMEE) integrates domain knowledge and machine learning components as configurable operators embedded in directed acyclic graph pipelines, enabling structured experimentation and lifecycle management within a unified platform. As a first representative hybrid strategy, residual learning is implemented and demonstrated in a real-world...

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The paper focuses on human oversight mechanisms in Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems and their pipelines, with particular attention to Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) and Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) approaches. As a first representative hybrid strategy, residual learning is implemented and demonstrated in a real-world...

item.title: A Holistic Market Research Analysis of Carbon-Negative Fuel Systems embedding Decision-Making Procedures
item.description:
Authors: Fatima Dargam, Gabriel Steffen

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The document is a short paper for ICDSST 2026 is authored by REACH Innovation together with several European research partners and presents the Market and Competitive Research Analysis (MCRA) developed within the Horizon Europe project NET‑Fuels. The paper explains how decision-support methodologies (SWOT, PESTEL, Porter’s Five Forces) were integrated into a structured workflow to evaluate and position NET‑Fuels’ carbon‑negative fuel technologies within the current technological and regulatory landscape.

Associations with standardized cognitive scores were examined using mixed-effect models adjusted for potential confounders. Effect modification by sex, education, and smoking status was explored in subgroup analyses. RESULTS: and working memory were modified by the smoking status and education level. CONCLUSION: to poorer visual working memory.

item.title: Service Science Framework for Frugal AI: A Design Science Approach for AI Adoption in SMEs
item.description:
Authors: Thang Le Dinh, Manh-Chiên Vu, Viet T. Dao

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) face structural barriers to artificial intelligence (AI) adoption, including limited budgets, data scarcity, and skill gaps, that render dominant resource-intensive AI paradigms inaccessible. This paper proposes the Service Science Framework for Frugal AI (SSFFAI), a Design Science Research artifact that integrates frugal AI engineering with Service-Dominant Logic to support cost-effective AI adoption in SMEs. SSFFAI defines four interdependent dimensions: value, management, science, and technology that are linked through a Support-Use-Enable logic that connects strategic objectives, business service systems, and lightweight AI techniques to the creation of multidimensional value. A four-stage co-creation method operationalizes the framework into actionable implementation guidance. Three illustrative instantiations demonstrate its applicability...

item.title: Spatial Spillover Effects of Virtual Queue Services on Local Businesses
item.description:
Authors: Hyeonju Ju, Jin Soo Han, Hyeokkoo Eric Kwon, G R Lee

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Managing wait times is a critical challenge in service industries and firm revenue. Virtual queue services have emerged as a strategic solution enabling customers to reserve their place in line via mobile platforms. Despite their growing adoption, the broader spatial implications for surrounding local businesses remain underexplored. This study examines spillover effects of virtual queue adoption on nearby businesses using a difference-in-differences framework applied to 288 restaurants in Busan, South Korea. Drawing on time geography theory, we propose that virtual queues reconfigure consumer mobility during waiting periods, directing foot traffic toward surrounding establishments. Our results show that virtual queue adoption significantly increases sales in neighboring areas up to 950 meters away, substantially beyond the 300 meter range documented in prior literature. These...

item.title: Discovering Hidden Gems: AI-Human Complementarity in Content Selection on Webtoon Platforms
item.description:
Authors: Minah Kang, Hangjung Zo

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Editorial screening on content platforms operates under a structural mismatch: submission volume exceeds the capacity for careful human review. This study examines whether AI-based quality evaluation surfaces such overlooked content by providing information distinct from the market signals editors rely on. Analyzing 405 webtoon titles with archival promotion data (2013-2024) and multidimensional AI scoring, we find that AI evaluation and pre-promotion reader ratings capture largely non-overlapping aspects of content value, and their combination accounts for editorial decisions more effectively than either alone. We identify 137 Hidden Gems, works rated highly by AI but not promoted, whose quality profiles are comparable to those of promoted titles. Genre saturation reduces promotion chances, yet AI quality scores carry greater weight in more competitive genres. These findings reflect...

item.title: How the Source of AI Disclosure Shapes Psychologcial Mechanisms and User Responses
item.description:
Authors: Sunyoung Choi, Hangjung Zo

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study investigates how the source of AI disclosure—system-led versus social-led—shapes users’ psychological mechanisms and behavioral responses in a post-disclosure context. Drawing on Expectancy Violation Theory and Attribution Theory, this study hypothesizes that social-led disclosure triggers stronger negative reactions by evoking an integrity-based attribution, leading to greater expectancy violation, trust erosion, and a sense of betrayal, ultimately resulting in higher levels of Negative Word-of-Mouth toward the platform. To test the proposed model, a scenario-based between-subjects experiment was conducted via the CloudResearch platform. Preliminary findings from 60 participants indicate that social-led disclosure elicits greater trust erosion and a sense of betrayal compared to system-led disclosure. By conceptualizing the disclosure source as a distinct theoretical...

item.title: Stabilize or Destabilize? Token–Based Ownership under Legitimacy Shocks
item.description:
Authors: Kayoung Shin, Moksh Matta, Hyeokkoo Eric Kwon

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Digital platforms often use cash-like incentives to encourage user contributions, but such rewards can make contributor supply fragile and highly sensitive to reward value. To build a more stable contribution base, some platforms grant tokens economically tied to platform value. Yet it remains unclear whether this tokenized ownership stabilizes contributions even under an exogenous legitimacy shock. We leverage two novel datasets from the mainnet and the Q&A platform to examine this question. Using a Difference-in-Differences design, we find that total weekly rewards do not change significantly, but the composition of reward-earning activity shifts. Substantive contributions—including Q&A quantity and quality and referrals—decline, while attendance check-ins increase, despite being lower-value activities that still incur reward costs. Together, these findings advance understanding of...

item.title: HOW GREEN MOBILE APPS ENABLE SUSTAINABLE ACTION: A SUSTAINABILITY AFFORDANCE PERSPECTIVE
item.description:
Authors: Sepide Sadeghi, Ning Nan

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Green mobile applications have emerged as promising tools for encouraging people to act more sustainably in their daily lives, yet their ability to generate meaningful and sustained environmental impact remains limited. Despite growing user engagement, a persistent gap exists between digital participation and tangible behavioral change. Drawing on affordance theory, in this short paper, we analyzed 18 in-depth interviews with users of green mobile applications and identified six sustainability affordances that shape how digital features translate into real-world environmental behaviors. Our findings reveal that not all affordances are equal; while some directly drive offline sustainable behaviors, others primarily sustain digital engagement without triggering meaningful change. By mapping affordance valence and outcome types, we offer the first taxonomy of sustainability affordances for...

item.title: Authorship in Action: Learning from Pooh—Misunderstanding and Humor in Contemporary Picture Books
item.description:
Authors: Kari‐Lynn Winters

Source: Hollins Digital Commons (Hollins University)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Misunderstanding lies at the heart of Winnie-the-Pooh’s enduring appeal. Moving through the world with gentle misinterpretations, Pooh embodies a form of childlike logic that produces both quiet humor and subtle social complication. This presentation explores misunderstanding not as a deficit, but as a productive narrative and emotional space—one that generates pleasure, invites curiosity, and fosters connection. Drawing on close readings of Winnie-the-Pooh (Milne, 1926) alongside contemporary picture book authorship practices, this session considers how the tradition of “feeling small and misunderstood” evolves in recent humorous picture books. Focusing on picture books I have authored, What If I’m Not a Cat? (Winters & Collier, 2023) and Jeffrey and Sloth (Winters & Hodson, 2007), alongside other contemporary humorous texts, this presentation examines how misunderstanding operates...

item.title: After-Life Archetype
item.description:
Authors: Vanessa Santala

Source: Hollins Digital Commons (Hollins University)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This paper introduces the “After-Life Archetype” as a new model for understanding the resilience of children’s literature, arguing that survival is not an inherent quality of texts but a process shaped by material production, imaginative play, and cultural value. Moving beyond traditional bibliographic frameworks, it challenges the concept of children’s books not simply as texts but as dynamic objects that exist across a continuum of care, from production and marketplace circulation to child use, domestic preservation, and institutional archiving. In doing so, it reframes literary criticism as an inquiry not only into meaning but into the conditions that determine which books endure, and why. Central to this argument is the role of imaginative play. Children’s books function as toys, commodities, and experiential objects through their use by way of handling, marking, damaging, and...

item.title: Dining off Pooh: Recipes of White Childhood Innocence
item.description:
Authors: Donna Varga

Source: Hollins Digital Commons (Hollins University)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This presentation demonstrates how the White colonialist desire for maintaining childhood innocence as a dominant social paradigm is found in cookbook themes based on Euro-Anglo children’s literature that assure White adults that they can cook up a life free of adult discomforts. Also discussed is how these texts discursively shield Euro-Anglo admirers from acknowledging racist food connotations in children’s stories, such as found in the degradation imposed on the youth servant in Holy Molé!, and with his being characterized as the clumsy, stupid, accidental inventor of the traditional dish. In contrast are recipe-stories highlighting BIPOC defiance against colonialist oppression with narratives linking race, racism, and resistance. Juxtaposing these against ones that reiterate an imagined White virtuousness as embedded in the likes of Winnie-the-Pooh cookbooks expose how White centric...

item.title: Virtual Exchange as an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion-compliant approach to English Language Teacher education
item.description:
Authors: Orsini-Jones, Marina; id_orcid 0000-0001-5250-5682, Chen, Yu-Hua; id_orcid 0000-0002-6350-6426, Koseoglu, Guray, Mkpayah, Patience, Suri, Preeti; id_orcid 0009-0001-3786-377X, Zhang, Kai

Source: Pure (Coventry University)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This chapter discusses how virtual exchange (VE) projects can support female students on English language teacher education programmes, whose first language is not English, with reflecting on their beliefs, approaching their professional development in a critical way and feeling included and valued. Focusing in particular on the autoethnographical reflections of female participants who took part in VE projects that aimed at decolonising the English Language Teaching (ELT) curriculum, the chapter illustrates how VE can provide a powerful knowledge-sharing third space that enables female students to co-construct otherwise and pluralised ways of learning while also developing useful transversal competences.
